label: Chapter-12.ppt
title: Chapter Chapter  12 Classes and Data Abstraction

questions.text: In C++, what is the default access specifier for members of a `class`?
questions.explanation: By default, all members of a class are private unless specified otherwise by the public or protected keywords.

questions.text: Which of the following is a requirement for a class definition in C++?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It must contain at least one constructor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It must end with a semicolon after the closing brace.

choices.label: C
choices.text: All members must be declared as public.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Memory is allocated as soon as the class is defined.

questions.explanation: A class definition syntax requires a semicolon (;) immediately following the closing curly brace.

questions.text: Which symbol is used in Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagrams to denote a **private** member?
questions.explanation: In UML diagrams, '-' denotes private, '+' denotes public, and '#' denotes protected members.

questions.text: What type of value does a constructor return?

choices.label: D
choices.text: A constructor has no return type.

questions.explanation: Constructors have no return type, not even void, and their name must be the same as the class name.

questions.text: Which of the following built-in operators is valid for class objects by default (without overloading)?
questions.explanation: Most built-in operations do not apply to classes. However, member access (.) and assignment (=) are valid built-in operations.

questions.text: What is the purpose of using `const` in a member function heading, such as: `void print() const;`?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To ensure the function returns a constant value.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To prevent the function from modifying the class's member variables.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To allow the function to access private members.

questions.explanation: A constant member function (accessor) is declared with 'const' to guarantee it will not modify any data members of the object.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding **static** member variables of a class?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Each object has its own copy of the static variable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Static variables exist only after an object is instantiated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Static variables exist even if no objects of the class are created.

questions.explanation: Static member variables are shared among all objects and exist even if no objects of the class type exist.

questions.text: What is a **Mutator** function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A function that deletes an object.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A function that only reads the values of member variables.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A function that modifies the value(s) of member variables.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A function that is declared as static.

questions.explanation: Mutator functions are member functions specifically designed to modify or update the values of private data members.

questions.text: In C++, the only difference between a `struct` and a `class` is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: A class can have member functions but a struct cannot.

choices.label: B
choices.text: By default, members of a struct are public, while members of a class are private.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no difference; they are identical in every way.

questions.explanation: Both have the same capabilities, but structs default to public access, whereas classes default to private.

questions.text: Which file extension is typically used for the **interface** (specification) of a class?
questions.explanation: Interface files (header files) use the .h extension, while implementation files use .cpp.
t1 calls the default constructor (A) and t2 calls the parameterized constructor (B), resulting in 'AB'.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The code runs successfully but does nothing.

questions.explanation: Member 'x' is private by default and cannot be accessed directly from outside the class (main).
The static variable 'count' is shared. Three objects are created, each incrementing the same variable, so the final value is 3.
The assignment operator (=) works by default on class objects by copying member variables. obj2 becomes a copy of obj1.
Destructors execute automatically when an object goes out of scope. The inner block ends before 'M' is printed, so the destructor (D) runs first.
p[0] is initialized with (1, 2). p[1] is initialized using the default constructor (0, 0) because no explicit value was provided.

choices.label: A
choices.text: The function show is not public.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Missing the scope resolution operator in Sample.cpp.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A class cannot have a function named show.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both A and B are errors.

questions.explanation: First, 'show' is private by default. Second, in Sample.cpp, it should be 'void Sample::show()' to link it to the class.
The object is passed by value to 'manipulate', meaning a copy is made. The changes to the copy do not affect the original object 'myS'.
v2 = v1 copies the value (10). Changing v1 afterwards does not change v2 because they are independent objects in memory.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compile error: d is treated as a function declaration.

questions.explanation: The line 'Demo d();' is interpreted by C++ as a declaration of a function named 'd' that returns a 'Demo' object. To call the default constructor, use 'Demo d;' without parentheses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compile error: Cannot call static function using ::

questions.explanation: Public static member functions can be accessed using the class name and the scope resolution operator (::) without needing an object.
C++ provides a default copy constructor if none is defined. b2 is initialized with b1's 's' value (4). 4 * 4 = 16.
A member function marked as 'const' cannot modify any member variables of the class. Line 2 attempts to change 'val'.
The constructor uses a default parameter. d1 uses the default (10), while d2 passes an explicit value (5).
When 'f()' is called, 'obj' is created (prints 1). When 'f()' finishes, 'obj' is destroyed (prints 2). Then main prints 3.
